attributes.stem: An 18-year-old man is brought in to the emergency department by his parents after injuring his left hand while playing basketball 1 hour ago. The ball hit his middle finger with significant force and he is unable to extend the end of his finger, but he did not hear or feel a crack. On examination, it is a closed injury and he is unable to extend his left middle finger at the distal interphalangeal joint due to pain, whereas finger flexion is still possible. An X-ray was done and showed no fractures.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management of this condition?
attributes.explanation:
Stack splint in hyperextension is the correct answer. Based on the mechanism of injury as well as the clinical presentation, this is characteristic of a tendinous mallet finger due to tendon injury. For an uncomplicated extensor digitorum tendon injury with an absence of a fracture, a stack splint is used to stabilise the join in slight hyperextension so that the opposing ends of the tendon can be brought together in close approximation for improved recovery. The splint is applied for 6-8 weeks and there should be caution when applying pressure to bony protuberances due to the risk of skin necrosis and subsequent impaired healing of the tendon. In addition, the finger should not be flexed at any point during the immobilisation period as the healing fibres would be disrupted, resulting in an extended period of immobilisation.

K-wire fixation involves the usage of metal wires to fixate bone fragments in a specific position with close approximation of the bone to improve fracture healing. However, since this patient most likely has a tendon injury with no fractures detected on X-ray imaging, this management option is not indicated.

Tendon repair via open surgery is rarely indicated in closed tendon injuries, even if there are associated fractures. The main indications for open surgery are an open injury (penetrating injury or open fracture), displaced bony mallet with subluxation, or if the patient cannot wear a splint due to occupational reasons. In addition, conservative measures such as stack splinting is always preferred before surgical treatment unless the above mentioned indications are present.

Buddy taping is one of the conservative management options in tendon injuries, especially those that require immobilisation. However, this method is not appropriate for an acute mallet finger injury as it cannot ensure that the injured finger is held in hyperextension for appropriate tendon approximation and recovery, unlike a stack splint. Despite this, buddy taping can be still be used in specific circumstances after initial therapy, such as athletes requiring finger flexion for their sport.

Tenodermodesis is a procedure which utilises sutures to maintain the distal phalange in hyperextension. It is usually used to treat chronic mallet finger deformities (swan neck deformity) instead of an acute injury, and is a last line option when conservative measures for mallet finger have been exhausted. Swan neck deformity is also treated with a double ring splint before tenodermodesis is indicated.
learningPoints: If untreated, mallet finger can result in a swan neck deformity with due to chronic DIPJ flexion and PIPJ hyperextension.
references: Smit JM, Beets MR, Zeebregts CJ, Rood A, Welters CFM. Treatment options for mallet finger: a review. Plast Reconstr Surg. 2010 Nov;126(5):1624-1629. doi: 10.1097/PRS.0b013e3181ef8ec8. PMID: 21042117. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21042117/

Bendre AA, Hartigan BJ, Kalainov DM. Mallet finger. J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2005 Sep;13(5):336-44. doi: 10.5435/00124635-200509000-00007. PMID: 16148359. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16148359/

Yee J, Waseem M. Mallet Finger Injuries. 2023 Aug 7. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan–. PMID: 29083648. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29083648/
